panel.origin: Driven by terpene alcohols like geraniol and beta-phenylethanol. These delicate compounds evaporate early during wood extraction, meaning intense floral characteristics indicate minimized, hyper-judicious new oak contact or highly specialized micro-distallation curves.
panel.mixology: Frame this for your guests as an elegant 'perfumed aromatic brightness' typical of single-distillate column entries or white grape focus. Beautiful for a floral variation on a French 75 or Sidecar.
panel.examples: Commonly seen in exceptionally elegant Borderies Cognacs and young, floral Armagnacs from the Bas-Armagnac region.

panel.origin: Indicates partial oxidative concentration of terpene pools interacting with minor levels of aldehydes over extended timeframes. It signals a complex evolution intermediate between direct floral bloom and oxidative dried fruit.
panel.mixology: Describe this as a 'deep, dried botanical warmth.' Excellent for pairing tableside with lighter, fruit-driven dessert configurations.
panel.examples: Often detected in complex, long-aged Grande Champagne Cognacs.

panel.origin: Direct varietal carryover of free monoterpenes (linalool and geraniol). Unlike ugni blanc, which distills neutral, muscat-derived base wines hold high terpene fractions that survive pot-still execution without thermal breakdown.
panel.mixology: An absolute winner for aromatic curiosity. Tell guests this delivers the rich, explicit perfume of fresh table grapes directly to the glass. Perfect baseline for an aromatic brandy punch.
panel.examples: A distinctive marker in South African Pot Still Brandies, where Muscat d'Alexandrie (locally known as Hanepoot) is utilized.

panel.origin: Arises from the combination of grape-derived concentrated sugars and cask absorption of high-density residual sugar left behind in seasoned oak pores. Thermal changes in the copper still can also amplify light furfural-caramelization mirroring sun-dried fruit.
panel.mixology: A very welcoming, accessible note. Tell guests it represents 'concentrated, sundried grape richness.' Instantly direct them to try it neat as a post-dinner replacement for sweet wines.
panel.examples: Immensely typical in Spanish Brandy de Jerez aged in Pedro Ximénez casks.

panel.origin: An overproduction of volatile fatty acid esters, particularly isoamyl acetate (banana drop) and ethyl butyrate (pineapple candy). Often signals a lack of precise head cutting or temperature control during primary yeast conversions.
panel.mixology: If a guest notes an artificial or 'bubblegum-like' fruit character, this is the compound. It brings a candy lift that works well when trying to cut through heavy syrups in high-volume crushed ice tiki modifications.
panel.examples: Encountered in lower-tier continuous column distillates or high-speed, hot industrial fermentations.

panel.origin: A complex sign of micro-oxidation interacting with wood lactones over decades. This breakdown product of fatty acid structures mimics concentrated stone-and-seed fruits as the liquid concentrates inside porous oak.
panel.mixology: Describe this as a rich, savory jamminess. Excellent option to recommend alongside charcuterie boards featuring cured duck or sheep's milk cheeses.
panel.examples: Prominent in well-matured XO Cognacs and traditional, rancio-forward Armagnacs.

panel.origin: Driven by ethyl malate and hexyl acetate. High-acid varieties preserve apple-pith crispness throughout distillation. If heavy and stewed, it points to higher temperatures; if green, it tracks to sharp, pristine primary cuts.
panel.mixology: A crisp, refreshing baseline marker. Use this asset to construct crisp, seasonal variations of a classic Jack Rose or highball with artisanal soda.
panel.examples: Ubiquitous across Calvados (apple brandy) and young, vibrant cognacs using high-acid base wines.

panel.origin: Driven by light limonene monoterpene trails. These volatile elements are highly interactive with copper surfaces; their cleanly delineated execution requires highly targeted, sharp heart captures on the still.
panel.mixology: Points to a bright, vibrant, zest-like character. Great baseline indicator to match with orange liqueurs for the ultimate premium Sidecar script.
panel.examples: Frequently found in Fins Bois and Bons Bois Cognacs, providing a bright top note lift.

panel.origin: Derived from prolonged interaction with localized dark wood tannins and minor, legal, traditional use of boisé (oak-water reduction cooked with prune elements) to achieve integration in old casks. Represents a structural marker of rich, heavy distillates.
panel.mixology: Deep, dark, stewed plum warmth. Point guests toward this option if they are seeking a bold, velvety spirit to accompany heavy espresso or dark chocolate pairings.
panel.examples: A signature classic trait of older, traditional Armagnacs from Bas-Armagnac.

panel.origin: The pure, primary expression of the base crop untouched by wood chemistry. Highlights simple hexanol chains and fresh fruit esters surviving low-temperature copper still conversions.
panel.mixology: The raw essence of the fruit before oak alters its profile. Highly recommended for crisp, aromatic, refreshing summer serves like a Pisco Sour.
panel.examples: Vibrant and clear in clear Piscos and unaged Eau-de-Vie de Vin.

panel.origin: Driven by the emergence of aromatic trace gamma-lactones. These develop as wood lignin starts breaking down, combining beautifully with the concentrated residual white-wine fruit properties of the grape blend.
panel.mixology: A sophisticated stone-fruit note. Use this profile to upsell your guest to a mid-tier VSOP selection served over a large clear ice rock.
panel.examples: Classic secondary indicator in mid-aged VSOP and early XO expressions.

panel.origin: Tracks directly to original varietal esters and early stage lactone synthesis during temperature-monitored, long yeast fermentations.
panel.mixology: Fresh, juicy, summer orchard brightness. An accessible gateway note for guests transitioning from white wines or gin to aged brown spirits.
panel.examples: Apparent in young, high-quality copper pot still brandies before aggressive wood pickup.

panel.origin: Sourced from native vegetation terpene variables or specific storage interactions. Contains specific sulfur-terpene arrays (diosphenol), yielding a distinctive blackcurrant-mint complexity unique to the cape.
panel.mixology: An exotic, minty botanical note. Perfect for high-level cocktail curation where you need a savory, aromatic accent to stand out.
panel.examples: Highly specific to South African Pot Still brandies utilizing regional fynbos-adjacent setups.

panel.origin: Driven by trace capture of 1,8-cineole. It adds an ethereal, cooling sensation on the upper palate that cuts through heavy, sweet oak compounds.
panel.mixology: A cooling, mint-like freshness. Suggest this option to guests who appreciate a bright, non-cloying structure in their premium spirits.
panel.examples: Spotted across specific New World expressions where nearby flora interacts with open-air fermentation vats.

panel.origin: Volatile monoterpenoid formations captured at high, early rectifying markers. Provides an elevated lifting sensation on the nose.
panel.mixology: A clean, cooling lift. Excellent for refreshing summer highballs modified with premium tonic or mineral waters.
panel.examples: Found in specific high-acid, cool-vintage distillates or particular column extractions.

panel.origin: Driven by aldehyde compounds like hexanal. Indicates early harvest profiles or high canopy shade during grape cluster maturation.
panel.mixology: A fresh, green, snapping herbal property. Good for adding a crisp vegetal dimension in modern mixology configurations.
panel.examples: Occasionally noticeable in young, cold-fermented base wine distillates.

panel.origin: Signals heavy amino acid tracking or minor levels of thermal scorching during wash distillation inside the kettle.
panel.mixology: A warm, cereal, grain-like depth. Perfect bridge recommendation for guests who generally drink Scotch or Bourbon.
panel.examples: Present in specific heavy continuous pot-still variations or direct cereal-grain blended variations.

panel.origin: The oxidative breakdown of carotenoids inside the spirit over years of barrel storage, yielding subtle beta-damascenone elements that mimic dried leaves and field straws.
panel.mixology: A beautifully elegant, rustic maturity. Point your serious spirits collectors toward this profile for a reflective neat pour experience.
panel.examples: A signature tertiary character of gracefully aged, old vintage cognacs and armagnacs.

panel.origin: Indicates a high release of structural wood fiber elements without corresponding vanillin or toast chemicals, often due to over-used barrels.
panel.mixology: If a guest finds the finish excessively dry or 'wood-shaving' heavy, it stems from old wood extraction. Balance it with rich sweeteners in cocktails.
panel.examples: Found in spirits stored in old, exhausted wood or improperly seasoned casks.

panel.origin: Direct extraction of vanillin from the thermal degradation of wood lignin during the barrel toasting process. More pronounced in newer oak barrels.
panel.mixology: A universal crowd pleaser. Tell guests this luxurious note comes naturally from oak contact. Suggest it as an ideal introductory neat pour or premium base.
panel.examples: The classic calling card of high-grade VSOP and XO selections aged in seasoned French oak (Limousin/Tronçais).

panel.origin: An encompassing structural extraction of oak tannins, wood sugars, and volatile aromatics. Forms the physical and texturized backbone of aged distillates.
panel.mixology: The core foundational signature of barrel aging. Gives the liquid its weight, structural dry finish, and golden color profile.
panel.examples: Found across all standard aged brandy categories.

panel.origin: Complex combination of oxidized resinous terpenes and deep cask wood extractions, showing up late in the maturation spectrum.
panel.mixology: An elite, deeply satisfying aromatic note reminiscent of fine humidor boxes. Best served entirely neat to let the aroma open up slowly.
panel.examples: Spotted in ancient, ultra-aged vintage selections with extended wood contact.

panel.origin: Derived from high-temperature wood cell crystallization during Cooperage breakdown, liberating rich, sap-adjacent aromatic compounds.
panel.mixology: A bold, slightly sappy wood depth. Adds an incredible structural spine that holds its own perfectly against heavy bitters in an Old Fashioned format.
panel.examples: Often tracked in heavy toast barrel regimes or brandies using highly specific wood varieties.

descriptors.name: Cigar box/tobacco pouch
descriptors.name_fr: Boîte à Cigares / Blague à Tabac
descriptors.name_it: Scatola di Sigari / Borsa del Tabacco

panel.origin: A harmonious convergence of wood extraction, natural grape rancio, and micro-oxygenation. Represents advanced degradation of complex cask compounds.
panel.mixology: The pinnacle of aged complexity. Describe this to your guest as a warm, aromatic spiced-tobacco leaf profile. Must be enjoyed neat in proper glassware.
panel.examples: The ultimate benchmark tertiary aroma of prestigious, old XO and Hors d'Âge Cognacs.

panel.origin: Driven by Maillard reaction browning products like furfurals generated when intense flame caramelizes wood sugars during barrel manufacturing.
panel.mixology: A dark, roasted espresso-like warmth. An incredible bridge option to suggest to guests who typically drink dark, charred Bourbons.
panel.examples: Noticeable in heavy-toast barrel executions or brandies aged in re-charred casks.

panel.origin: Traces back to guaiacol and 4-methylguaiacol absorbed from wood smoke during structural barrel-making preparation.
panel.mixology: A distinct toasted element. Great for adding a sophisticated, faintly campfire-tinged complexity to spirit-forward cocktail builds.
panel.examples: Found in expressions utilizing direct oak fire toasting or specific charred barrel finishes.

panel.origin: Tracks to intermediate oxidation cycles interacting with light wood lactone compounds, creating a creamy, rounded fat profile.
panel.mixology: A smooth, highly inviting nuttiness. Pairs flawlessly with dessert courses or rich, milk-chocolate pairings.
panel.examples: Commonly encountered in well-integrated, elegant VSOP Cognacs.

panel.origin: Linked to trace paths of benzaldehyde. When subtle and clean, it adds an elegant marzipan top-note lift to the primary nose.
panel.mixology: A sweet, marzipan-like nuance. Gives an excellent textural lift that cuts through fresh lemon juice beautifully in a traditional sour.
panel.examples: Vibrant in younger, pit-still expressions and brandies undergoing traditional lees distillation.

panel.origin: Signals deliberate, highly styled oxidative maturation columns. The interaction with air develops distinct rancio attributes mirroring oxidized nut oils.
panel.mixology: A dry, classic savory nut flavor. Perfect for presenting as an alternative option for guests who typically gravitate towards dry sherries or tawny ports.
panel.examples: A major, classic component of highly developed, older Spanish Solera brandies.

panel.origin: Direct extraction of systemic acetals and oxidative yeast indicators absorbed from old cask stave structures saturated with flor yeast culture.
panel.mixology: A highly specialized style choice. It brings a crisp, dry, complex savory attribute that makes an incredible substitute for dry vermouth in high-end variations.
panel.examples: Exclusively found in Brandy de Jerez aged in traditional casks previously holding Fino or Manzanilla sherry.

panel.origin: Result of dark wine extraction from oak wood pores, pulling rich anthocyanin properties and deep berry wine properties into the brandy solution.
panel.mixology: Delivers a rich, dark-fruit wine finish. Highlight this option for guests who want a sweeter, smoother profile with a velvety texture.
panel.examples: Encountered in modern brandies featuring targeted finish treatments in vintage Port wine casks.

panel.origin: Tracks to heavy cask wood seasoning saturation from Oloroso or Pedro Ximénez sherry fills, introducing high unctuousness and specific sugar paths.
panel.mixology: Rich, luscious, and deeply comforting. An easy recommendation for anyone who prefers a rounder, sweet-tinged style over sharp, dry spirits.
panel.examples: Standard baseline markers in classic Spanish Brandy de Jerez Solera systems.

panel.origin: High level concentration of deep furfural tracks interacting with dense cask sugars over prolonged warm-climate maturation.
panel.mixology: A thick, dark, dark-caramel depth. Excellent for cold weather menus or mixed into bold, hot beverage variations.
panel.examples: Noticeable in heavily toasted barrel treatments or where slight wood sugar caramelization is maximized.

panel.origin: Complex chemical synthesis of pyrazines extracted from deep wood toast interacting over long timeframes with decomposing wine esters.
panel.mixology: An indulgent, bittersweet cocoa note. Point guests toward this selection as an excellent liquid dessert alternative at the end of the evening.
panel.examples: Frequently highlighted in luxurious, ancient XO Cognacs and premium long-aged pot still selections.

panel.origin: Can stem from highly caramelized barrel sugar extractions or legal standard additions of spirit caramel used to adjust consistency across batch volumes.
panel.mixology: A highly approachable, buttery caramel sweetness. A fantastic baseline option to build smooth, balanced classic cocktail presentations.
panel.examples: Common across highly integrated consumer selections and brandies using small, controlled dose additions of caramel color.

panel.origin: Driven by phenylacetic acid esters. Surfacing frequently when floral terpenes start oxidizing cleanly without heavy oak wood masking.
panel.mixology: A rich, organic sweetness on the nose. Beautiful for adding an elegant, texturized lift to warm toddy formats or premium sours.
panel.examples: Evident in aromatic distillates, particularly those showing early stage noble-rot interactions or highly floral base crops.

panel.origin: High concentrations of furaneol compounds combining with primary red grape ester residue, creating a distinct cooked preserve note.
panel.mixology: A playful, sweet berry conserve trait. Fun to utilize as an interesting talking point for adventurous cocktail menu themes.
panel.examples: A unique, sweet fruit note found occasionally in specific warm-climate distillates.

panel.origin: Derived from wood-extracted cinnamaldehyde. Extracted steadily from toast arrays, it indicates clean wood structural integration.
panel.mixology: Warm, baking-spice comfort. Highlight this selection to guests on cool autumn or winter evenings as an inviting neat pour option.
panel.examples: Highly visible in brandies using medium-plus toast or new oak integration phases.

panel.origin: Direct extraction of eugenol from the seasoned oak wood structures. This compound peaks around medium toast levels during coopering.
panel.mixology: A sharp, aromatic baking spice element. Gives the spirit a great structural edge that stands up well against rich culinary pairings.
panel.examples: Commonly associated with brandies matured in tightly grained European oak casks.

panel.origin: A complex spice profile arising from the integration of wood eugenol paths with micro-oxidized terpene alcohol fractions over time.
panel.mixology: A classic, elegant spice attribute. Ideal for highlighting in classic variations like the Brandy Alexander or festive eggnog formats.
panel.examples: Frequently found in balanced, well-aged VSOP and older traditional representations.

panel.origin: Signals contaminated winemaking mechanics or lazy tank rinsing procedures prior to loading raw base crop materials.
panel.mixology: An immediate rejection note. If a guest identifies a soapy, chemical cleanser attribute, pull the glass, verify the bottle, and replace immediately.
panel.examples: Fault indicator. Tracks to industrial cleaner residues in processing tanks or fermentation vats.

panel.origin: Ethyl acetate forms via the condensation of acetic acid and ethanol. At trace tiers, it gives a fruity lift; at high volume, it yields a piercing model-airplane glue fault.
panel.mixology: A distinct chemical fault resembling harsh glue. If detected, the spirit is fundamentally flawed and should be removed from service.
panel.examples: Fault indicator. Found in poorly managed, wild-ferment setups or distressed distillations.

panel.origin: A collection of ultra-low boiling point fractions including direct acetone properties. These should be strictly separated and removed during the initial still execution phase.
panel.mixology: Smells explicitly like nail polish remover. A structural distillation error that completely ruins the mid-palate and nose. Do not serve.
panel.examples: Fault indicator. Appears when head cuts on the still are taken far too late in the distillation curve.

panel.origin: Raw, unrefined alcohol vapor masking underlying fruit properties. Indicates insufficient aging time to allow ester integration or poor proofing down execution.
panel.mixology: If a guest complains that the brandy feels too 'hot' or burns the nose, the ethanol vapor is unintegrated. Serve it on ice to calm the burn.
panel.examples: Noticeable in young, unintegrated, cheap high-proof column selections.

panel.origin: Result of alcohol oxidation, generating high concentrations of acetaldehyde. Mimics raw bruised apples or flat, oxidized white wine notes.
panel.mixology: Brings a sharp, bruised-apple staleness. Signals either an unjudicious distillation cut or severe oxygen exposure failure in the cellar.
panel.examples: Fault indicator. Common in oxidized base wines or spirits with excessive head integration.

panel.origin: Acetobacter contamination in the base crop converts ethanol to vinegar properties, which then distills over, throwing off the structural balance.
panel.mixology: A sour, sharp vinegar odor. This indicates an absolute failure of raw material quality control before distillation even began.
panel.examples: Fault indicator. Tracks to infected, vinegary base wines entering the still.

panel.origin: High volatile acidity presence tracking cleanly through the system due to inadequate monitoring of fermentation tank seals.
panel.mixology: A sour, stale wine properties note. Lacks the clean finish expected of a polished, pristine commercial spirit selection.
panel.examples: Fault indicator. Found in low-tier brandies using highly unstable, turning base wines.

panel.origin: Bacterial generation of butyric acid, creating highly offensive sensory compounds that mimic spoiled butter or human vomit.
panel.mixology: An absolute critical failure note. If encountered, the batch is completely contaminated. Do not allow this to reach a guest table.
panel.examples: Fault indicator. Highly problematic, stemming from bacterial infections during late-stage fermentation.

panel.origin: Heavy, sharp organic compounds tracking over due to aggressive boiling speeds inside the pot still, confusing the heart cuts.
panel.mixology: A harsh, burning, prickly sensation that overpowers the nasal passages. Indicates poor technical handling on the still.
panel.examples: Fault indicator. Associated with dirty distillation runs or late feint inclusions.

panel.origin: A collection of high-molecular-weight alcohols (isoamyl, propanol, butanol). Necessary in tiny trace increments for texture, but toxic and oily if over-extracted.
panel.mixology: Brings a heavy, thick, industrial oil character that coats the throat unpleasantly. Use with caution.
panel.examples: Found in heavy, unrefined spirits or brandies with lazy tail separation boundaries.

panel.origin: Long-chain fatty acids tracking into the final collection. Can create visual cloudiness or oily textures when proofed down with water.
panel.mixology: A heavy, slightly waxy, thick aroma. If it cuts down the vibrant fruit profile too much, it points to a lazy tail cut by the distiller.
panel.examples: Noticeable when tail management limits are pushed too far into the low-proof runoff.

panel.origin: Heavy nicotine-adjacent and alkaloid compounds condensed late in the run sequence. Pushes a bitter, dark, heavy tar property.
panel.mixology: A heavy, slightly acrid tobacco leaf ash character. Distinct from the beautiful 'cigar box' oak notes; this feels dirty and heavy.
panel.examples: Encountered in deep tail cuts on old-school pot stills.

panel.origin: Advanced decomposition of nitrogenous elements inside distressed base wash setups, concentrating foul amine streams into the collection.
panel.mixology: An immediate disqualifier. Smells explicitly of stale fish or decomposing ocean proteins. Pull from inventory immediately.
panel.examples: Severe fault indicator. Stemming from amine compound spoilage or major tank cleaning issues.

panel.origin: When executed with master-level care, heating dead yeast cells releases beautiful creaminess and structural complexity. If mismanaged, it yields an autolytic yeast staleness.
panel.mixology: A heavy, bready, yeasty compound. In artisan selections, it adds incredible body; in cheap setups, it just tastes heavy and cloudy.
panel.examples: Typical in traditional method Cognacs distilled 'sur lie' (on the yeast sediment).

panel.origin: Cellulose breakdown tracking into the solution, masking delicate floral top notes with a flat dryness.
panel.mixology: A flat, dry, dull recycled-paper note. Strips the spirit of its natural vibrancy and leaves the finish boring and short.
panel.examples: Fault indicator. Found in spirits stored in cheap packaging or filtering systems.

panel.origin: Driven by 2,4,6-Trichloroanisole (TCA). Even at single-digit parts per trillion, it strips out all beautiful oak and fruit chemistry, leaving a damp cellar smell.
panel.mixology: The absolute calling card of a corked bottle. If a guest says it smells like a 'wet cardboard box,' validate their claim instantly and replace the pour.
panel.examples: Severe fault indicator. The classic sign of systemic cork taint or tainted storage lines.

panel.origin: Driven by 1-octen-3-ol. If elegant and old, it acts as a forest-floor accent; if young, it points to moldy, botrytis-damaged grapes entering the crush pad.
panel.mixology: An earthy, raw fungal character. If it overpowers the fruit in a young brandy, it points to poor fruit sorting standards during harvest.
panel.examples: Can show up as a tertiary note in old cellars, or indicate rot issues on the grape clusters.

panel.origin: Microbial contamination within the natural cork structure generating localized off-odors that seep down into the neck of the bottle.
panel.mixology: A variant of TCA taint. If the nose feels muted and stale, inspect the cork disc for physical mold or dry rot signatures.
panel.examples: Fault indicator. Localized packaging failure on individual bottle enclosures.

panel.origin: Molds or anaerobic bacteria nesting inside un-topped or improperly dried empty barrels, ruining any spirit later filled inside.
panel.mixology: A permanent stale wood property that cannot be aerated or fixed with decanting. The entire bottle allocation is compromised.
panel.examples: Fault indicator. Stemming from old, infected wood storage systems in the cellar.

panel.origin: Excess accumulation of volatile fatty acids like isovaleric acid, creating a distinct locker-room or stale sweat profile.
panel.mixology: A stale, human sweat off-note. Highly disruptive to clean tasting routines. Reject the bottle if this persists after open aeration.
panel.examples: Fault indicator. Associated with wild yeast strains or late distillation selections.

panel.origin: Stressed yeast lacking nitrogen inputs generates excessive H₂S (rotten egg gas). If the still lacks adequate copper contact, it fails to strip it out.
panel.mixology: Smells clearly of rotten eggs or struck matches. Points to an anaerobic failure in fermentation or a still lacking clean copper surfaces.
panel.examples: Fault indicator. The result of stressed yeast tracking through an un-cleansed distillation process.

panel.origin: Excessive vineyard or crush-pad SO₂ additions gas off during heating, creating a sharp, stinging sensation in the throat.
panel.mixology: A sharp, prickly, match-stick sting on the nose. It causes an immediate throat catch that ruins any elegant neat service delivery.
panel.examples: Fault indicator. Stemming from over-sulfited grape juice entering the still system.

panel.origin: Decomposition of sulfur-containing amino acids yielding dimethyl sulfide (DMS). Pushes notes of cooked or rotting vegetation.
panel.mixology: Smells distinctly of boiled cabbage or canned corn. A clear industrial distraction that shouldn't be present in a premium selection.
panel.examples: Fault indicator. A standard sulfur compound error from late distillation phases.

panel.origin: Conversion of simple sulfides into heavy ethanethiol structures, generating permanent foul, garlic-like properties.
panel.mixology: An absolute culinary distraction in spirits service. It smells like raw garlic oil or rubber gas. Unfit for commercial cocktail use.
panel.examples: Severe fault indicator. Pushed by highly advanced reduction setups or mercaptan conversions.

panel.origin: Heavy, sharp organic compounds tracking over due to aggressive boiling speeds inside the pot still, confusing the heart cuts.
panel.mixology: A heavy, chemical tar pitch. Overpowers all base grape notes and feels highly industrial and sharp on the back palate.
panel.examples: Fault indicator. Tracks to over-heated, scorched pot still bottoms or industrial sealing failures.

panel.origin: High-proof spirit acting as a solvent on sub-standard industrial synthetic hoses, leaching polymer chemicals into the final liquid.
panel.mixology: An industrial chemical fault. If a guest notes a 'synthetic plastic tupperware' flavor, it stems from chemical leaching. Do not serve.
panel.examples: Fault indicator. Caused by improper storage lines or cheap plastic hose connections in production plants.

panel.origin: Spirit interaction with non-inert metal systems, picking up free iron ions that catalyze fast oxidation and leave a bloody taste.
panel.mixology: A sharp, tinny, blood-like finish. It leaves a metallic weight on the sides of the tongue that destroys the entire mid-palate structure.
panel.examples: Fault indicator. Stemming from prolonged storage in raw metal transit vats or old iron pipe setups.

panel.origin: Absorption of external heavy hydrocarbon vapors during the open vat wine stage, or complex ring-compound synthesis under heat.
panel.mixology: Smells like raw machinery fuel or oily garage floors. A catastrophic production contamination flaw that requires discarding the bottle.
panel.examples: Fault indicator. Tracks to exhaust gas leaks near open fermentation tanks or severe reduction paths.

panel.origin: Yeast sediment drops to the bottom of the copper kettle and bakes directly onto the hot metal, releasing bitter furfurals and acrid carbon notes.
panel.mixology: A bitter, burnt, ash-tray finish. Signals a lack of technical care during the high-heat distillation phase by the distillery crew.
panel.examples: Fault indicator. Caused by running direct-fired pot stills too fast without checking solids.

panel.origin: Advanced synthesis of complex vulcanized rubber sulfides formed when heavy tail fractions interact with high heating elements.
panel.mixology: Smells explicitly of burnt car tires or old elastic bands. A heavy, industrial distraction that completely ruins mixology balance.
panel.examples: Fault indicator. A deep, heavy sulfur reduction error.

panel.origin: Driven by diacetyl synthesis from lactic acid bacteria activity. Delightful in tiny traces for texture, but greasy if unmonitored.
panel.mixology: A heavy, movie-popcorn butter quality. If it feels too slick or artificial, it will distract from clean cocktail acid structures.
panel.examples: Can show up in white wine distillates that completed full Malolactic conversion.

panel.origin: High percentage presence of fixed higher alcohols and essential fatty acid lipids that alter surface tension variables on the palate.
panel.mixology: A thick, mouth-coating weight. Great if you want a bold texture to hold up to heavy modifiers, but cloying if served warm neat.
panel.examples: Found in spirits with heavy tail collection limits or low-rectification column curves.

panel.origin: Advanced ester synthesis generated when fatty acids in dead yeast slurry undergo thermal binding with ethanol, creating rich lipid weights.
panel.mixology: A unique, fatty, luxurious waxy fruitiness. Highly prized by spirits connoisseurs for adding deep, structural length to the finish.
panel.examples: Classic secondary character in artisan charente pot still methods.

panel.origin: Hydrolysis of heavy fats generating free sodium-soap structures when high-proof elements interact with residue cleanser layers.
panel.mixology: A flat, filmy, candle-wax or soap lather profile. It leaves a slick, flat clean taste that strips out any genuine flavor length.
panel.examples: Fault indicator. Stemming from extreme tail extensions or bad tank washing setups.

FLORAL.en: Floral notes — rose, potpourri, Muscat — come from the base wine and grape variety, and are most vivid in young, unaged brandies.
FLORAL.es: Las notas florales — rosa, popurrí, Moscatel — provienen del vino base y la variedad de uva, y son más vívidas en los brandies jóvenes y sin añejar.

FRUITY.en: Fruity notes — apple, grape, dried apricot — are the fingerprint of the base wine. Fresh fruit suggests a younger spirit; dried or cooked fruit suggests barrel age.
FRUITY.es: Las notas afrutadas — manzana, uva, chabacano seco — son la huella digital del vino base. La fruta fresca sugiere un destilado más joven; la fruta seca o cocida sugiere añejamiento en barrica.

HERBACEOUS.en: Herbaceous notes — mint, eucalyptus, hay — often come from the grape variety or herbs growing near the vineyard, and can also signal a still-spirity greenness in young brandy.
HERBACEOUS.es: Las notas herbáceas — menta, eucalipto, heno — a menudo vienen de la variedad de uva o de hierbas cercanas al viñedo, y también pueden señalar un carácter verde y de destilado en un brandy joven.

WOODY.en: Woody notes — oak, vanilla, cedar — are the clearest marker of barrel aging. The longer a brandy rests in cask, the more these notes build.
WOODY.es: Las notas amaderadas — roble, vainilla, cedro — son el indicador más claro del añejamiento en barrica. Cuanto más tiempo descansa un brandy en barril, más se desarrollan estas notas.

TOASTED / NUTTY.en: Toasted and nutty notes — coffee, hazelnut, almond, sherry-like 'flor' — come from barrel char and oxidative aging, common in older brandies aged in ex-sherry casks.
TOASTED / NUTTY.es: Las notas tostadas y de frutos secos — café, avellana, almendra, 'flor' tipo jerez — provienen del tostado de la barrica y del añejamiento oxidativo, comunes en brandies más viejos en barricas ex-jerez.

SWEET ASSOCIATED.en: Sweet-associated notes — caramel, honey, chocolate, port-like jamminess — develop with extended barrel age as wood sugars and Maillard reactions build richness.
SWEET ASSOCIATED.es: Las notas asociadas a lo dulce — caramelo, miel, chocolate, mermelada tipo Oporto — se desarrollan con un añejamiento prolongado en barrica, conforme los azúcares de la madera y las reacciones de Maillard aportan riqueza.

SPICES.en: Spice notes — cinnamon, clove, nutmeg — come from oak compounds released during toasting, adding warmth and complexity to aged brandy.
SPICES.es: Las notas de especia — canela, clavo, nuez moscada — provienen de los compuestos del roble liberados durante el tostado, y añaden calidez y complejidad al brandy añejado.

SOLVENT/CHEMICAL.en: This whole group is a FAULT. Detergent, acetone, or nail-polish-like notes mean the distillation cut was poorly made or the spirit is unrested — they should be faint or absent in a well-made brandy.
SOLVENT/CHEMICAL.es: Todo este grupo es un DEFECTO. Las notas a detergente, acetona o quitaesmalte significan que el corte de la destilación se hizo mal o que el destilado no ha reposado — deberían ser tenues o estar ausentes en un buen brandy.

SOUR ASSOCIATED.en: Sour notes — vinegar, rancid butter, fusel oil — usually come from the 'heads' and 'feints' of distillation or microbial spoilage in the base wine. A little can add complexity; a lot is a fault.
SOUR ASSOCIATED.es: Las notas ácidas — vinagre, mantequilla rancia, aceite de fusel — suelen provenir de las 'cabezas' y 'flemas' de la destilación o de un deterioro microbiano en el vino base. Un poco puede añadir complejidad; mucho es un defecto.

TAILS.en: 'Tails' notes — tobacco oil, fishy, lees — come from the heavier compounds collected at the end of distillation. A trace can add savory depth; too much is heavy and unpleasant.
TAILS.es: Las notas de 'colas' — aceite de tabaco, a pescado, lías — provienen de los compuestos más pesados recogidos al final de la destilación. Un rastro puede añadir profundidad sabrosa; en exceso resulta pesado y desagradable.

MUSTY.en: Musty notes — wet cardboard, mushroom, corked barrel — are FAULTS pointing to a contaminated cask or cork, and dull the brandy's fruit and aroma.
MUSTY.es: Las notas a humedad — cartón mojado, hongo, barrica con corcho defectuoso — son DEFECTOS que apuntan a un barril o corcho contaminado, y apagan la fruta y el aroma del brandy.

SULPHURY.en: Sulphur notes — rotten egg, cooked cabbage, garlic — are FAULTS from fermentation stress or excess sulphur dioxide, and usually need to 'blow off' before tasting.
SULPHURY.es: Las notas de azufre — huevo podrido, col cocida, ajo — son DEFECTOS de una fermentación estresada o un exceso de dióxido de azufre, y normalmente necesitan 'disiparse' antes de catar.

PHENOLIC / TAR.en: Phenolic and tar notes — plastic, diesel, scorched, rubber — are FAULTS from over-toasted barrels or distillation problems, giving a harsh, industrial edge.
PHENOLIC / TAR.es: Las notas fenólicas y de brea — plástico, diésel, quemado, hule — son DEFECTOS de barricas demasiado tostadas o problemas de destilación, que dan un filo áspero e industrial.

SMOOTH ASSOCIATED.en: Smooth-associated notes — butter, oil, soap — in small amounts add a silky mouthfeel from lees contact and oak fats; in excess they feel waxy or soapy.
SMOOTH ASSOCIATED.es: Las notas asociadas a lo suave — mantequilla, aceite, jabón — en pequeñas cantidades añaden una textura sedosa por el contacto con las lías y las grasas del roble; en exceso se sienten cerosas o jabonosas.
